examples.title: Cohen-Sutherland Line Clipping
examples.description: Implementation of Cohen-Sutherland algorithm with outcode calculation and line clipping
examples.code:
// Constants for outcode bits
CONSTANT INSIDE = 0 // 0000
CONSTANT LEFT = 1 // 0001
CONSTANT RIGHT = 2 // 0010
CONSTANT BOTTOM = 4 // 0100
CONSTANT TOP = 8 // 1000

FUNCTION computeOutcode(x, y, xmin, ymin, xmax, ymax):
 code = INSIDE
 IF x < xmin THEN code = code | LEFT
 IF x > xmax THEN code = code | RIGHT
 IF y < ymin THEN code = code | BOTTOM
 IF y > ymax THEN code = code | TOP
 RETURN code

FUNCTION cohenSutherlandClip(x1, y1, x2, y2, xmin, ymin, xmax, ymax):
 outcode1 = computeOutcode(x1, y1)
 outcode2 = computeOutcode(x2, y2)
 
 WHILE NOT (outcode1 == 0 AND outcode2 == 0):
 IF (outcode1 AND outcode2) != 0 THEN
 RETURN FALSE // Trivial reject
 END IF
 
 // Choose endpoint with non-zero outcode
 IF outcode1 != 0 THEN outcode = outcode1 ELSE outcode = outcode2
 
 // Find intersection and clip
 IF (outcode AND TOP) != 0 THEN
 x = x1 + (x2-x1) * (ymax-y1)/(y2-y1)
 y = ymax
 ELSE IF (outcode AND BOTTOM) != 0 THEN
 x = x1 + (x2-x1) * (ymin-y1)/(y2-y1)
 y = ymin
 ELSE IF (outcode AND RIGHT) != 0 THEN
 y = y1 + (y2-y1) * (xmax-x1)/(x2-x1)
 x = xmax
 ELSE IF (outcode AND LEFT) != 0 THEN
 y = y1 + (y2-y1) * (xmin-x1)/(x2-x1)
 x = xmin
 END IF
 
 // Update outcode and endpoint
 IF outcode == outcode1 THEN
 x1 = x; y1 = y; outcode1 = computeOutcode(x1,y1)
 ELSE
 x2 = x; y2 = y; outcode2 = computeOutcode(x2,y2)
 END IF
 END WHILE
 
 RETURN TRUE // Trivial accept
examples.explanation: The algorithm computes 4-bit outcodes for line endpoints based on their position relative to the clipping window. Lines completely inside (0000) are accepted, lines with AND ≠ 0 are rejected. Other lines are clipped by computing intersections with window boundaries.

examples.title: Window-to-Viewport Transformation
examples.description: Mathematical transformation to map window coordinates to viewport coordinates
examples.code:
FUNCTION windowToViewport(xw, yw, wxmin, wxmax, wymin, wymax, vxmin, vxmax, vymin, vymax):
 // Step 1: Normalize window to [0,1]
 sx = (wxmax - wxmin)
 sy = (wymax - wymin)
 
 xn = (xw - wxmin) / sx
 yn = (yw - wymin) / sy
 
 // Step 2: Scale to viewport size
 vxsize = vxmax - vxmin
 vysize = vymax - vymin
 
 xv = xn * vxsize + vxmin
 yv = yn * vysize + vymin
 
 RETURN (xv, yv)

// Combined transformation formula:
sx = (vxmax - vxmin) / (wxmax - wxmin)
sy = (vymax - vymin) / (wymax - wymin)
xv = sx * (xw - wxmin) + vxmin
yv = sy * (yw - wymin) + vymin
examples.explanation: The transformation consists of translation to window origin, scaling by the ratio of viewport to window sizes, and translation to viewport position. This maintains relative positions while adapting to different screen areas.

examples.title: Liang-Barsky Line Clipping
examples.description: Parametric line clipping algorithm that is more efficient than Cohen-Sutherland
examples.code:
FUNCTION liangBarskyClip(x1, y1, x2, y2, xmin, ymin, xmax, ymax):
 // Line parametric form: P(t) = P1 + t(P2-P1)
 // 0 <= t <= 1 for the line segment
 
 dx = x2 - x1
 dy = y2 - y1
 
 p1 = -dx; q1 = x1 - xmin
 p2 = dx; q2 = xmax - x1
 p3 = -dy; q3 = y1 - ymin
 p4 = dy; q4 = ymax - y1
 
 // Initialize parameters
 u1 = 0.0; u2 = 1.0
 
 // Check each boundary
 IF p1 != 0 THEN
 r = q1 / p1
 IF p1 < 0 THEN u1 = MAX(u1, r) ELSE u2 = MIN(u2, r)
 ELSE IF q1 < 0 THEN RETURN FALSE // Line parallel and outside
 
 IF p2 != 0 THEN
 r = q2 / p2
 IF p2 < 0 THEN u1 = MAX(u1, r) ELSE u2 = MIN(u2, r)
 ELSE IF q2 < 0 THEN RETURN FALSE
 
 IF p3 != 0 THEN
 r = q3 / p3
 IF p3 < 0 THEN u1 = MAX(u1, r) ELSE u2 = MIN(u2, r)
 ELSE IF q3 < 0 THEN RETURN FALSE
 
 IF p4 != 0 THEN
 r = q4 / p4
 IF p4 < 0 THEN u1 = MAX(u1, r) ELSE u2 = MIN(u2, r)
 ELSE IF q4 < 0 THEN RETURN FALSE
 
 // Check if valid clip exists
 IF u1 > u2 THEN RETURN FALSE
 
 // Compute clipped endpoints
 x1Clip = x1 + u1 * dx
 y1Clip = y1 + u1 * dy
 x2Clip = x1 + u2 * dx
 y2Clip = y1 + u2 * dy
 
 RETURN (x1Clip, y1Clip, x2Clip, y2Clip)
examples.explanation: Liang-Barsky uses the parametric form of lines and computes clipping parameters directly. It clips against all four boundaries in a single pass, making it more efficient than Cohen-Sutherland especially for rectangles.

examples.title: Perspective Projection Matrix
examples.explanation: The perspective projection matrix creates the foreshortening effect by dividing x and y coordinates by -z. The w component becomes -z, and perspective division creates the depth illusion. Field of view and aspect ratio control the viewing angle and image proportions.
